lines.text: Alright, we're {{ELAPSED}} in and the East Side is *hot*. So let's talk about anxiety, shall we? Green Day. 'Basket Case.' From Dookie. 1994.
Billie Joe Armstrong wrote this about actual panic attacks. His father died when he was ten—fragmented the whole family. You don't come back from that clean.
They recorded Dookie in just over three weeks at Fantasy Studios in Berkeley. Three weeks. And they made the most important pop-punk album of all time. Sold twenty million copies worldwide.
Armstrong's lyrics—he touched on boredom, anxiety, mass murder, divorce, masturbation. Everything uncomfortable. Everything real. That's why Dookie hit different.
Basket Case. Still sounds like panic. Still sounds like you're losing it. Perfect for midnight on Purge Night.
NOFX. 'Linoleum.' 1996. From Punk in Drublic—their fifth album, peaked at number twelve on the Heatseekers chart and went gold six years later.
Fat Mike, Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin—those three are on every NOFX record ever made. They formed in LA in '83 at Fairfax High School, named themselves after a Boston hardcore band called Negative FX.
Fat Mike made a choice with the 'Leave It Alone' video from this same album. They shot it, looked at it, and decided: we're not sending this to MTV. We don't want to be part of that machine.
That's the punk move. That's the move nobody makes anymore. NOFX never signed to a major label. Never took the deal. They stayed clean.
And somehow—*somehow*—they became one of the biggest punk bands alive. Linoleum. Listen to this.
The Descendents. 'Hope.' 1982. Off Milo Goes to College—their debut. And yes, Milo Aukerman actually went to UC San Diego. He actually went to college.

The Descendents came out of Manhattan Beach, formed in '77. They took punk and made it *sweet*. Beach Boys influences. Songs about girls, parents, food, getting rejected. Cheeky love songs disguised as hardcore blasts.

Here's the thing that breaks me: one of the original guys, Frank Navetta, left during I Don't Want to Grow Up to become a fisherman in Oregon. The other guy, Tony Lombardo, left after that—touring was incompatible with his job as a postal carrier.
Can you imagine? You're in one of the greatest punk bands ever, and you go back to delivering mail. That's commitment to something else. That's real.
Hope. This one's for the people barricaded on Atwood right now. For Darren, if you're listening.
Alkaline Trio. 'Stupid Kid.' 2000. From Maybe I'll Catch Fire. Chicago band, formed in late '96 by Matt Skiba and Rob Doran.
Matt and Dan Andriano—they're co-lead vocals. Dark lyrics. Catchy hooks. Fast tempos. Alcoholism, depression, shattered relationships, drugs, death. The whole menu.
Before Goddamnit came out in '98, these guys couldn't quit their day jobs. They were bike messengers. Record store clerks. Living the dream but not the sustainable way.
This song—'Stupid Kid'—it became one of their breakout singles. It charted. They made it to radio. And they did it with lyrics about being broken and confused and too young to understand any of it.
I know the feeling. I think we all know the feeling tonight.
Anti-Flag. 'Die for the Government.' 1996. From A New Kind of Army—1999 release on Go-Kart Records addressing abortion, corruption, racism, fascism, police brutality, unity amongst American youths.

Justin Sane and Pat Thetic formed Anti-Flag in Pittsburgh in '88. They built a band on fervent anti-war activism, criticism of US foreign policy, corporatism, wealth distribution. Politics as the spine.

Here's what kills me: Anti-Flag signed to RCA Records in 2006 and dropped For Blood and Empire. The punk community *exploded*. Accusations of selling out everywhere. They'd spent their whole career saying corporations are the enemy. Then they signed to one.
But they stayed political. They kept making the same argument. And Fat Mike—who formed Fat Wreck Chords and never signed to a major label—*never* called them out. The respect was there.
That's what this song is. Die for the Government. Question everything.

tracks.title: Crazy
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Crazy.' 1997. From Have a Ball—their debut, recorded at One Week Records in San Francisco.

These are supergroup guys. Spike Slawson from Swingin' Utters. Fat Mike from NOFX. Joey Cape from Lagwagon. Chris Shiflett from No Use for a Name—also from Foo Fighters. They formed to cover songs during off-time from their main acts.

The Gimmes don't write originals. They cover everything and punk it into oblivion. Have a Ball was all '60s and '70s pop. They did this one about being insane—which, listen, at midnight on Purge Night, who's not insane?
In the liner notes, Fat Mike got so caught up in one of their songs that he thought he was Fat Stevens, a guitar-toting Islamic Extremist. The man was having a full identity crisis in the studio.
That's punk. That's what happens when you don't take yourself seriously for a single second. Crazy. Perfect. Let it play.

tickerItems.text: East Side kill count now at 34 cumulative. Atwood, Tenney Park, Schenk's Corners holding hot. {{ELAPSED}} in.
Willy Street barricade still holding. Yahara River bridge secured—neighborhood watch reports zero breach attempts past hour.
PurgeShield Panels: Your second skin. One caller reported installation error earlier. Check yours now. PurgeShield: We answer at any hour.
